description: Watson, returning from a patient visit, passed through Baker Street and was seized with a desire to see Holmes.
Watson looked up at Holmes's brilliantly lit rooms and observed Holmes's tall figure pacing back and forth in silhouette against the blind.
Watson rang the bell, was shown up to Holmes's chamber, and the two were reunited.
Holmes was summoned to Odessa in connection with the Trepoff murder case.
Holmes cleared up the singular tragedy of the Atkinson brothers at Trincomalee.
Holmes accomplished a delicate and successful mission for the reigning family of Holland.
Holmes deduced that Watson had put on seven and a half pounds since they last met.
Watson corrected Holmes's weight estimate, saying it was seven pounds, not seven and a half.
Holmes deduced that Watson had returned to medical practice.
Holmes deduced that Watson had recently been caught in very wet weather and had a clumsy servant girl, by observing scrape marks on Watson's left shoe.
Watson took a country walk on Thursday and came home in a dreadful mess.
Watson changed his clothes after returning muddy from his country walk.
Watson's wife gave Mary Jane notice of dismissal.
Holmes chuckled to himself and rubbed his long, nervous hands together.
Holmes explained the reasoning behind his deductions about Watson's wet walk, clumsy servant, and medical practice.
Watson laughed at the ease with which Holmes explained his process of deduction.
Holmes lit a cigarette and threw himself down into an armchair.
Holmes challenged Watson to state how many steps lead from the hall to his room, illustrating the difference between seeing and observing.
Holmes reveals he knows there are seventeen steps to their rooms because he has both seen and observed them.
Holmes threw a sheet of thick pink-tinted notepaper across to Watson for him to examine.
Watson read the anonymous undated note aloud at Holmes's request.
Watson carefully examined the handwriting and the paper of the mysterious note.
Watson held the notepaper up to the light and observed a watermark with letters 'E', 'g', 'P', 'G', and 't' woven into the paper.
Holmes took down the Continental Gazetteer from his shelves and consulted it to identify the origin of the paper.
Holmes deduced that the paper was made in Bohemia and that the letter-writer was German.
A carriage arrived outside with the sound of horses' hoofs and wheels against the curb, followed by a ring at the bell.
Holmes urged Watson to stay rather than leave, saying he was lost without his Boswell.
A slow and heavy step paused outside Holmes's door and a loud authoritative tap was heard.
A tall, richly dressed man wearing a black vizard mask entered Holmes's rooms.
Holmes caught Watson by the wrist and pushed him back into his chair, insisting the visitor speak before both of them.
Watson rose to leave the room when the visitor expressed preference for privacy.
The Count Von Kramm bound Holmes and Watson to absolute secrecy for two years, citing the matter's potential influence on European history.
Holmes and Watson each promised absolute secrecy to the visitor.
The visitor admitted that his agent identity was concealed and that the title 'Count Von Kramm' was not truly his own.
Holmes dryly acknowledged that he was already aware the visitor's title was false.
The disguised King explains that the circumstances implicate the House of Ormstein and a potential immense scandal.
Holmes announces he already knew he was addressing Wilhelm Gottsreich Sigismond von Ormstein, Grand Duke of Cassel-Felstein and hereditary King of Bohemia.
The King tore the mask from his face and hurled it on the ground, revealing his true identity.
The King sprang from his chair and paced up and down the room in uncontrollable agitation.
The King came incognito from Prague to consult Sherlock Holmes.
Some five years ago, during a lengthy visit to Warsaw, the King made the acquaintance of Irene Adler.
Watson looked up Irene Adler's biography in Holmes's index at Holmes's request.
Holmes read aloud key facts from Irene Adler's biography including her birthplace, birth year, operatic career, and current London residence.

Holmes systematically dismisses each piece of evidence the King cites as proof of authenticity for the letters (writing, note-paper, seal, photograph), arguing each could be forged, stolen, or imitated.
The King reveals that he and Irene Adler were both in the compromising photograph together, alarming Holmes.
The King admits he was mad and young when he committed the indiscretion, noting he is now only thirty.
Irene Adler refused to sell the photograph when approached.
Five attempts were made to recover the photograph from Irene Adler: two burglaries of her house, one diversion of her luggage during travel, and two attempts to waylay her.
Holmes laughed upon hearing about the failed attempts to retrieve the photograph.
The King reveals he is about to be married to Clotilde Lothman von Saxe-Meningen.
Irene Adler threatens to send the compromising photograph to the King's betrothed on the day the betrothal is publicly proclaimed.
Holmes tells the King he will drop him a line at the Langham Hotel to report progress.
The King granted Holmes carte blanche and confirmed he would give a province of his kingdom to recover the photograph.
The King took a heavy chamois leather bag from under his cloak and laid it on the table.
Holmes scribbled a receipt upon a sheet of his note-book and handed it to the King.
Holmes noted down Irene Adler's address at Briony Lodge, Serpentine Avenue, St. John's Wood.
Holmes asked whether the photograph was a cabinet and the King confirmed it was.
Holmes bid the King good-night and invited him to call the following afternoon at three o'clock.
Watson arrived at Baker Street at three o'clock precisely the following afternoon but found Holmes had not yet returned.
Watson sat down beside the fire to await Holmes's return.
Close upon four o'clock, Holmes returned to Baker Street disguised as a drunken-looking ill-kempt groom, unrecognised at first by Watson.
Holmes vanished into the bedroom and emerged five minutes later dressed in his usual tweed suit.
Holmes stretched out by the fire and laughed heartily for several minutes.
Holmes left his house disguised as an out-of-work groom to conduct surveillance.
Holmes located and closely examined Briony Lodge from every point of view.
Holmes discovered a mews in a lane running along one wall of Briony Lodge's garden.
Holmes lent the ostlers a hand rubbing down their horses and gathered information about Irene Adler and the neighbourhood in exchange.
Watson asked Holmes what information he had obtained about Irene Adler.
After listening to the ostlers, Holmes began walking up and down near Briony Lodge again to plan his campaign.
Holmes learned from the cabmen that Irene Adler's frequent male visitor is Godfrey Norton of the Inner Temple.
Godfrey Norton arrived at Briony Lodge by hansom cab, shouted to the cabman to wait, and entered the house hurriedly.
Godfrey Norton spent about half an hour inside Briony Lodge, pacing and talking excitedly in the sitting-room.
Godfrey Norton emerged from Briony Lodge, checked his gold watch, and ordered the cabman to drive to Gross & Hankey's and then to the Church of St. Monica in twenty minutes.
Irene Adler rushed out of Briony Lodge and into a hastily prepared landau, ordering the coachman John to drive to the Church of St. Monica in twenty minutes.
Sherlock Holmes hailed a passing cab and directed the driver to the Church of St. Monica, offering half a sovereign for arrival within twenty minutes.
Holmes's cab arrived at the Church of St. Monica to find Norton's cab and Irene Adler's landau already there with steaming horses.
Watson paid the cab driver and hurried into the Church of St. Monica.
Watson entered the side aisle and observed the clergyman expostulating with Norton and Adler in front of the altar.
Godfrey Norton ran towards Watson after the three at the altar turned to face him.
Watson was dragged to the altar and served as a witness, helping to legally marry Irene Adler to Godfrey Norton.
Irene Adler gave Watson a sovereign after the wedding ceremony.
At the church door, Norton drove back to the Temple and Adler went to her own house in separate directions.
Holmes rang the bell and requested cold beef and a glass of beer, explaining he had been too busy to eat.
Holmes asked Watson for his co-operation in the evening's plans.
Sherlock Holmes explains his plan of action to Dr. Watson over the tray, detailing what will happen at Briony Lodge.
Dr. Watson agrees to assist Sherlock Holmes and accept a neutral role in the plan, promising not to interfere.
Holmes took a plumber's smoke-rocket from his pocket and explained its use to Watson.
Holmes instructed Watson on his role: stay neutral, approach the window, throw the smoke-rocket on signal, cry fire, and wait at the corner of the street.
Holmes disappeared into his bedroom and returned disguised as an amiable Nonconformist clergyman.
Holmes and Watson left Baker Street at a quarter past six and arrived in Serpentine Avenue ten minutes later.
Holmes and Watson paced up and down in front of Briony Lodge waiting for Irene Adler to arrive.
Holmes reasoned aloud to Watson about where Irene Adler would conceal the photograph, concluding she would not carry it on her person.
Two attempts were already made to waylay and search Irene Adler.
Holmes reasons aloud that the photograph must be hidden in Irene Adler's own house.
Holmes declares his plan to get Irene Adler to show him where the photograph is hidden.
Irene Adler's carriage arrived at Briony Lodge.
Holmes's accomplices staged a fierce street brawl as Irene Adler stepped from her carriage, drawing her into the centre of the struggle.
Holmes dashed into the crowd to protect Irene Adler, then cried out and fell to the ground with blood running down his face.
After Holmes fell, the guardsmen and loungers fled in different directions while bystanders crowded in to help the lady and attend to the injured man.
Irene Adler hurried up the steps of Briony Lodge but paused at the top to look back into the street.
Irene Adler asked from the top of the steps whether the injured gentleman was badly hurt.
Several voices in the crowd cried out that the injured man was dead.
Another bystander shouted that there was still life in the injured man.
A woman bystander praised the injured man's bravery and noted he had prevented a robbery.
Bystanders asked Irene Adler's permission to carry the injured man into her house.
Irene Adler gave permission for the injured man to be brought into her sitting-room and directed people to the sofa.
Holmes was carried into Briony Lodge and laid out on the principal room's couch.
The lamps in Briony Lodge had been lit but the blinds had not been drawn, allowing Watson to observe Holmes on the couch.
Irene Adler waited upon the injured Holmes with grace and kindliness, causing Watson to feel ashamed of conspiring against her.
Watson hardened his resolve and took the smoke-rocket from under his ulster.
Holmes sat up on the couch and motioned as if in need of air.
A maid rushed across the room and threw open the window.
Watson tossed the smoke-rocket into the room and cried 'Fire!' at Holmes's hand signal.
The entire crowd of spectators joined in a general shriek of 'Fire!'
Thick clouds of smoke curled through the room and out of the open window.
Holmes called out from within the house assuring the crowd that the fire was a false alarm.
Watson slipped through the shouting crowd and made his way to the corner of the street.
Watson rejoiced to find Holmes and walk away with him from the scene of uproar within ten minutes.
Holmes and Watson walked swiftly in silence down a quiet street towards the Edgeware Road after leaving the scene.
Holmes praised Watson for his performance during the staged fire alarm.
Holmes revealed to Watson that he knows where Irene Adler's photograph is located, though he does not have it in hand.
Irene Adler unwittingly revealed the hiding place of the photograph by instinctively moving toward it during the false fire alarm.
Holmes explained to Watson that all the people in the street were accomplices engaged for the evening.
Holmes concealed moist red paint in his palm, then fell down and pressed it to his face to feign injury during the staged commotion.
The crowd carried the apparently injured Holmes into Irene Adler's sitting-room, which was the room he had suspected held the photograph.
Holmes motioned for air and those present were compelled to open the window, giving Watson his opportunity to throw in the smoke-rocket.
Holmes explained that when a woman believes her house is on fire her instinct drives her to the thing she values most, a principle he has exploited before.
Holmes deduced that Irene Adler's photograph was hidden in a recess behind a sliding panel above the right bell-pull.
Irene Adler rushed to secure the photograph in its hiding place and half-drew it out when the fire alarm was raised.
Irene Adler replaced the photograph, glanced at the rocket, and rushed from the room after Holmes announced a false alarm.
Holmes rose, made his excuses, and escaped from Irene Adler's house.
Holmes hesitated and decided not to secure the photograph immediately because the coachman was watching him narrowly.
Holmes announced his plan to call with the King and Watson at eight in the morning to retrieve the photograph.
Holmes resolved to wire to the King without delay.
Holmes and Watson reached Baker Street and stopped at the door.
A slim youth in an ulster passing on the pavement called out 'Good-night, Mister Sherlock Holmes.'
Watson slept at Baker Street that night.
The King of Bohemia rushed into the room while Holmes and Watson were having breakfast with toast and coffee.
Holmes, Watson, and the King of Bohemia descended and set off by brougham for Briony Lodge a second time.
The King of Bohemia revealed to Holmes that Irene Adler had married an English lawyer named Godfrey Norton the previous day.
The brougham drew up in Serpentine Avenue and the party arrived at Briony Lodge.
An elderly woman standing at the open door of Briony Lodge addressed Holmes by name, revealing that her mistress had forewarned her of his likely visit.
Irene Adler and her husband left England on the 5:15 train from Charing Cross for the Continent.
Sherlock Holmes staggered back, white with chagrin and surprise, upon hearing that Irene Adler had left England.
Holmes pushed past the servant and rushed into the drawing-room, followed by the King and Watson.
Holmes rushed at the bell-pull, opened a sliding shutter, and retrieved a photograph and a letter hidden inside.
Holmes tore open Irene Adler's letter and all three — Holmes, the King, and Watson — read it together.
After the fire alarm, Irene Adler realised she had been deceived by Holmes and betrayed herself.
Irene Adler sent her coachman John to watch Holmes, then went upstairs and changed into her walking clothes (male costume).
Irene Adler, in disguise, followed Holmes to his door to confirm she was an object of his interest.
Irene Adler imprudently wished Holmes good-night and departed for the Inner Temple to see her husband.
Irene Adler and her husband decided that flight was the best course when pursued by Holmes, and resolved to leave.
Holmes, Watson, and the King of Bohemia all three read Irene Norton's letter together.
The King of Bohemia cried out in admiration of Irene Adler after reading her letter.
The King slipped an emerald snake ring from his finger and offered it to Holmes as a reward.
Holmes declined the ring and instead requested Irene Adler's photograph as his reward.
The King agreed to give Holmes the photograph of Irene Adler.
Holmes bowed, turned away without taking the King's outstretched hand, and left with Watson.
Watson narrates in summary that the great scandal threatening Bohemia was resolved and Holmes's plans were beaten by Irene Adler's wit.
Holmes stopped making merry over the cleverness of women after being outwitted by Irene Adler.
Holmes began referring to Irene Adler under the honourable title of 'the woman' when speaking of her or her photograph.
